name: L-shape mansion silhouette
description: A large residence whose footprint forms a right-angled L: two rectangular wings of unequal length meet at a corner, creating a longer primary wing and a shorter secondary wing perpendicular to it. The inner corner of the L naturally encloses a partial courtyard or forecourt, while the outer angle presents two distinct facades to the approaching visitor. Typical of mansions, manor houses, and large farmhouses, the silhouette breaks the monotony of a rectangular block and gives each wing its own roof, often with ridges running along each wing's long axis so the two roofs meet in a valley at the corner. The main entrance sits either at the inner corner — facing the courtyard — or centered on the primary wing's long facade.
placement_rules: Lay out two rectangular wings sharing a single right-angle corner; primary wing is the longer of the two, secondary wing is medium length.
Aspect of each wing should stay between 1.5 and 3.0 (length:width) so neither reads as a square block.
Roof ridges run along each wing's long axis; the two ridges meet at the inner corner forming a valley (or a hip if styles call for it).
Wing heights should match (same eave line) so the two roofs read as a single composition; mismatched heights are reserved for explicit tower-plus-hall variants.
Place the main entrance either at the inner corner facing the partial courtyard, or centered on the primary wing's long outer facade.
The inner corner of the L defines a partial courtyard — leave the ground clear there for paving, a garden, or a well; do not extend wings into it.

medieval.notes: Stone-and-timber manor; steep dark-wood roofs on both wings; inner courtyard paved with cobblestone.

renaissance.notes: Symmetrical L with matching wing widths; tiled roofs of equal pitch; formal courtyard with axial path to inner-corner entrance.

victorian.notes: Asymmetric brick manor with pronounced primary wing; gable on the short wing facing the courtyard; ornamented eaves and bay window at the inner angle.
